policy_statement: NAP target: telecommunications companies with adaptation or contingency plans for extreme events to reach 25% by 2020 and 100% by 2030.
mechanism_description: Increase the share of telecommunications companies with adaptation or contingency plans for extreme events to improve continuity of communications and resilience during disruptions.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme event) → contingency planning & continuity measures → ↓ service disruption / ↓ vulnerability of critical communications
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Telecommunications providers adopt and implement contingency/adaptation plans (e.g., redundancy, backup power, restoration protocols, mutual-aid arrangements, priority routing). (2) Mediating process: Improved operational preparedness and system redundancy increases the ability of telecom networks to withstand, absorb, and rapidly recover from hazard impacts (storms, heatwaves, floods, wildfires). (3) Outcome: Greater continuity of communications and reduced cascading failures in critical services during climate extremes, improving societal resilience and adaptive capacity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced disruption of critical communications during climate-related extreme events, lowering exposure/vulnerability and enabling emergency response and recovery.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards threaten telecom operations; contingency planning reduces disruption and vulnerability—an adaptation/resilience pathway. 2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while resilient communications can indirectly support mitigation actions, the direct and largest effect is maintaining service during extremes, not reducing GHGs (Mitigation) nor primarily reducing resource inputs (Resource Efficiency) nor enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions). 3) Edge cases applied: if contingency plans focus on energy efficiency or renewable backup generation, those are secondary co-benefits; the core mechanism remains continuity under hazards. 4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy whose mechanism is “increase telecom contingency/adaptation planning to ensure continuity during extreme events” primarily functions to reduce climate risk impacts, so Adaptation fits across contexts. 5) Note on secondary subtypes: any further tagging (e.g., critical infrastructure resilience, emergency management, digital resilience) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could include mitigation co-benefits if plans mandate low-carbon backup power or network energy optimization, but the dominant, mechanism-defining effect is continuity of service under climate extremes (adaptation).
key_indicators: “adaptation plans or contingency plans for extreme events”; “25%”; “100%”; “by 2020”; “by 2030”; “Telecommunications companies”
co_benefits: Improved general disaster preparedness and service reliability for non-climate shocks (e.g., technical failures, cyber incidents); reduced economic disruption from outages.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via regulation/licensing conditions or reporting mandates, but the verbatim text only evidences a quantified coverage target (no explicit mandate or enforcement mechanism), so classified as target-setting.
